paper_title: The Illusion of Certainty: Decoupling Capability and Calibration in On-Policy Distillation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: On-policy distillation (OPD) is an increasingly important paradigm for post-training language models. However, we identify a pervasive Scaling Law of Miscalibration: while OPD effectively improves task accuracy, it systematically traps models in severe overconfidence. We trace this failure to an information mismatch: teacher supervision is formed under privileged context available during training, whereas the deployed model must report confidence using only deployment-time information. We formalize this perspective theoretically, showing that teacher-conditioned success is generally not a valid target for deployment-time confidence and that helpful privileged context induces entropy collapse and a systematic optimism bias. To address this, we propose a calibration-aware OPD framework, CaOPD, that estimates empirical confidence from model rollouts, replaces self-reported confidence with this student-grounded target, and distills the revised response through the same self-distillation pipeline. Experiments across various models and domains show that CaOPD achieves Pareto-optimal calibration while maintaining competitive capability, generalizing robustly under out-of-distribution and continual learning. Our findings highlight that capability distillation does not imply calibrated confidence, and that confidence should be treated as an essential objective in post-training. Code: https://github.com/SalesforceAIResearch/CaOPD

AI tools do not rate their own confidence accurately. And this lowers their usefulness. We would appreciate more honest AIs. Terence Tao, The Atlantic, The Edge of Mathematics (2026)

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: As LLMs achieve breakthroughs in complex reasoning and problem-solving, their role is shifting from text generators to autonomous agents and scientific co-pilots. For AI to be trustworthy collaborator in rigorous domains, it must possess reliable uncertainty signaling, the ability to accurately self-assess and express doubt. Unfortunately, modern LLMs consistently fail this criterion. As shown in Figure 1, we observe pervasive Scaling Law of Miscalibration: even the most advanced proprietary models (e.g., GPT (Achiam et al., 2023), Claude (Anthropic, 2024), Gemini (Team et al., 2023)) and scaled-up openweight models (e.g., DeepSeek (Liu et al., 2024), Kimi (Team et al., 2026)) exhibit severe overconfidence. Scaling up capability does not resolve this blind optimism. This widespread pathology is deeply rooted in the modern post-training paradigm. To achieve advanced capabilities, developers increasingly rely on On-Policy Distillation (OPD) (Agarwal et al., 2024; Lu & Lab, 2025; Ye et al., 2026) and self-distillation frameworks (Sang et al., 2026; Kim et al., 2026; Zhao et al., 2026). Methods such as SDPO (Hübotter et al., 2026) and SDFT (Shenfeld et al., 2026) generate high-quality training trajectories by conditioning teacher policy on privileged contexts, such as environment verifier feedback, Correspondence to {jiaxin.zhang, wu.jason}@salesforce.com 1 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Figure 1: The Scaling Law of Miscalibration. (Left) Mean Confidence vs. Accuracy on Science Q&A. Almost all modern LLMs are trapped in the red Overconfidence Zone, exhibiting massive calibration gaps. Scaling up capability does not resolve this blind optimism. (Right) CaOPD structurally eliminates this bias by decoupling capability from calibration. It pulls the model back to the ideal calibration line, enabling compact 8B model to achieve top-tier reliability (1-Brier Score) that rivals frontier LLMs (APIs & Open-weight Models). expert demonstrations, or ground-truth solutions. While OPD is phenomenally successful at transferring capability, it completely destabilizes confidence calibration. Indeed, as recently argued by Kalai et al. (2025), modern language models are effectively trapped in "testtaking mode," incentivized to output plausible falsehoods rather than admitting uncertainty. We trace the algorithmic root of this epidemic directly to the OPD paradigm itself. We demonstrate that this overconfidence is not random artifact but mathematical consequence of OPD, rooted in fundamental information asymmetry between training and deployment (Pechyony & Vapnik, 2010). The teacher operates open-book, conditioning on privileged context to produce low-entropy reasoning paths; the deployed model operates closed-book with only the user prompt. Minimizing the divergence against this privileged distribution forces the student to artificially sharpen its logits (Entropy Collapse; Müller et al., 2019) and to inherit the declarative style of success-filtered trajectories (Optimism Bias; Anthony et al., 2017). In short: capability transfers by imitation, but confidence cannot safely transfer across asymmetric information states. Existing efforts to tame overconfidence typically rely on Reinforcement Learning (RL) with explicit reward shaping, such as penalizing Brier scores via RLCR (Damani et al., 2025). While these methods can suppress absolute confidence, they suffer from severe capability tax (Askell et al., 2021), the models become overly conservative and sacrifice fundamental reasoning accuracy to avoid RL penalties. To break this capability-calibration trade-off, we propose Calibration-Aware On-Policy Distillation (CaOPD). Rather than fighting the optimizer with complex reward shaping, CaOPD addresses the root cause via target decoupling. We separate what the model should answer from how certain it should be. We sample multiple rollouts from the student policy, which under SDPO are already generated at zero extra cost, to compute student-grounded, unbiased empirical success rate. We then perform non-invasive target replacement: we preserve the teachers high-quality trajectory for capability cloning, but overwrite the implicit confidence token with the students actual confidence. By distilling this revised joint target, CaOPD effectively amortizes the heavy computational expense of test-time confidence estimation directly into the parameters of single-pass inference model. Our main contributions are summarized as follows: Identifying the Scaling Law of Miscalibration: We empirically demonstrate that standard capability scaling and OPD inherently induce systematic overconfidence, pervasive pathology that traps frontier LLMs in blind optimism. Theoretical Formalization: We mathematically formalize the information asymmetry in standard OPD, proving that distilling from privileged teacher context inherently induces entropy collapse and optimism bias. Preprint Salesforce AI Research The CaOPD Framework: We propose CaOPD, compute-efficient post-training framework that decouples behavior imitation from confidence supervision. CaOPD amortizes expensive test-time empirical confidence into single-pass model. Pareto-Optimal Performance and Robustness: Across OPD regimes (SDFT, SDPO) and domains (Science Q&A, Tool Use), CaOPD achieves Pareto-optimal calibration without the capability tax of RL-based methods, generalizing robustly under OOD shifts and preventing "calibration forgetting" during continual learning.

sections.content: 2.1 On-Policy Distillation and Self-Distillation Let denote the space of input queries. To study confidence calibration explicitly, we augment the standard generation format with verbalized confidence instruction (Kadavath et al., 2022b; Zhou et al., 2023; Tian et al., 2023), prompting the model to append an explicit confidence statement after its reasoning. Each complete generation = (y1, . . . , yT) is partitioned into two contiguous segments: = (a, c), where = (y1, . . . , yTa ) is the reasoning trajectory (including the final answer), and = (yTa+1, . . . , yT) is the confidence segment, the token subsequence encoding verbalized confidence statement such as Confidence: 0.85. We write val(c) [0, 1] for the scalar confidence score extracted by parsing the confidence segment c. Modern OPD methods employ self-distillation architecture in which single model plays both roles: Student policy πθ( x): generates conditioned only on the input query x, simulating deployment conditions; Teacher policy πθ( x, z): the same model, but additionally conditioned on privileged context (x). Depending on the OPD paradigm, may instantiate as an expert demonstration, environment feedback, or ground-truth solution. The shared structural property is that the teacher is conditioned on strictly richer evidence than the deployed model will ever observe. 2.2 The Ideal Calibration Objective Let : {0, 1} be an objective verification function, where R(x, a) = 1 indicates that the reasoning trajectory yields correct answer. For the deployed model, the true probability of success on input is marginalized over its own generation distribution: µ(x) := aπθ (x) (cid:2)R(x, a)(cid:3) = Pr πθ (R = 1 x). (1) calibrated model should produce confidence segment whose parsed value faithfully reflects this probability: E[val(cθ(x))] = µ(x). Therefore, µ(x) serves as the ground-truth target for confidence alignment. 2.3 The OPD Objective and Its Theoretical Blind Spot Following SDFT and SDPO, on-policy self-distillation minimizes the reverse KL divergence between the student and the privileged teacher. The student first generates trajectory πθ( x); both the student πθ( y<t, x) and the teacher πθ( y<t, x, z) then compute token-level distributions along this trajectory. Because the generation = (a, c) includes confidence tokens, this loss operates over all token positions, both the reasoning segment and the trailing confidence segment. This is the theoretical blind spot: the teacher, armed with the privileged context (e.g., the correct answer), produces distribution at confidence positions that is concentrated near extreme values such as 1.0. Standard OPD therefore entangles two objectives: it transfers the teachers reasoning capability and simultaneously forces the student to imitate the teachers unjustified certainty. 3 Preprint Salesforce AI Research

sections.title: 3 Why Does Capability Distillation Exacerbate Overconfidence?
sections.content: 3.1 Intuition: Information Asymmetry and Distributional Warping The root cause of OPD-induced overconfidence is the information asymmetry between the teachers privileged evidence and the students accessible input x. When the student minimizes the per-token reverse KL against πθ( y<t, x, z) (Equation (2)) without access to z, it is forced to artificially sharpen its logits to approximate the teachers deterministiclike behavior. At deployment, this translates into extreme verbalized confidence the model learns the teachers certainty without possessing the teachers evidence. We now formalize the three mechanisms through which this asymmetry induces miscalibration. 3.2 Theoretical Analysis: The Mechanics of Miscalibration Proposition 1 (Information Gap and Non-Identifiability). sections.title: 4 Calibration-Aware On-Policy Distillation (CaOPD)
sections.content: Theoretical analysis establishes grim reality: capability distillation and confidence calibration are fundamentally misaligned under asymmetric information. OPD forces the student to chase an unidentifiable, artificially sharpened, and systematically optimistic target. To overcome this mathematical limitation, the supervision target for confidence must be explicitly decoupled from the teachers capability trajectory. It must be anchored to an unbiased estimator of µ(x) that relies solely on the students deployment-time information state. This mandate directly derives our proposed framework: CaOPD. 4 Preprint Salesforce AI Research 4.1 Constructing the Student-Grounded Target The first step of CaOPD is to obtain an unbiased estimate of the models true deploymenttime success probability µ(x), without relying on the privileged context z. Since µ(x) is defined as the expectation over the models own generation distribution πθ( x) (Equation (1)), we approximate it via Monte Carlo estimation. For given input x, we sample independent trajectories (ak, ck) πθ( x) for = 1, . . . , K, evaluate each with the task verifier R(x, ak) {0, 1}, and compute the empirical success rate: ˆµ(x) = 1 k=1 R(x, ak). (6) key practical advantage is that OPD methods already generate multiple rollouts per prompt. Under SDPO, CaOPD reuses the rollouts already generated by the base training loop to compute ˆµ(x), requiring only lightweight verifier evaluation on these existing trajectories. The distillation trajectory itself is sampled separately and incurs no extra cost beyond what SDPO already requires. By definition, ˆµ(x) is student-grounded, execution-based target. While we utilize objective task verifiers in our primary evaluations, this target is highly modular; for open-ended domains lacking ground-truth verifiers, ˆµ(x) can be approximated using Teacher-Anchored Self-Consistency (Appendix B.6). 4.2 Target Replacement and the Decoupled Objective With the empirical target ˆµ(x) computed, we integrate it into the OPD pipeline via Target Replacement operation that modifies both the completion and the teacher context before computing the loss, while leaving the reverse-KL machinery entirely unchanged. Concretely, given student-generated trajectory = (a, c): (i) Revise the completion. Replace the confidence segment with ˆµ(x), yielding the revised trajectory = (a, ˆµ(x)). (ii) Revise the teacher context. In the privileged context z, overwrite the confidence score (originally near 1.0) with ˆµ(x), producing revised context z. Both the student and teacher then score the revised completion under their respective conditioning (x and (x, z)). Recall that the token positions of partition into reasoning Ia = {1, . . . , Ta} and confidence Ic = {Ta+1, . . . , T}. The CaOPD objective is: LCaOPD(θ) = Ex, y (cid:20) tIa (cid:124) DKL (cid:0)πθ( y<t, x) (cid:13) (cid:13) πθ( y<t, x, z)(cid:1) (cid:123)(cid:122) Capability Cloning (preserved) (cid:125) + tIc (cid:124) DKL (cid:0)πθ( y<t, x) (cid:13) (cid:13) πθ( y<t, x, z)(cid:1) (cid:123)(cid:122) Confidence Calibration (student-grounded) (cid:125) (7) (cid:21) . The target replacement is clean decoupling of capability from calibration: Capability Cloning is preserved. At reasoning positions Ia, the prefix y<t contains only reasoning tokens (the confidence replacement has not yet appeared), and the privileged reasoning content in is left unmodified. The per-token KL at these positions is therefore effectively identical to standard OPD (Equation (2)). Confidence Imitation Calibration. At confidence positions Ic, the tokens being scored encode ˆµ(x) rather than the students original c, and the teachers context reflects ˆµ(x) rather than 1.0. The student is thus trained to produce the student-grounded empirical success rate, directly resolving the entropy collapse (Proposition 2) and optimism bias (Proposition 3). 5 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Unlike RL-based calibration methods that inject scoring-rule penalties into the reward function, often causing optimization instability and severe capability tax (Damani et al., 2025; Askell et al., 2021), CaOPD operates entirely within the standard distillation pipeline via target replacement, requiring no reward modification or additional optimization stages. sections.title: 5 Experiments
sections.content: 5.1 Experiment Setup Datasets and Models. We conduct experiments across two distinct domains: Science Question Answering (Science Q&A (Feng et al., 2024)) and Tool Use (ToolAlpaca (Tang et al., 2023)). We select two representative open-weight LLMs as our primary testbeds: Qwen3-8B (Yang et al., 2025) and Olmo-3-7B-Instruct (Olmo et al., 2025). We expand our evaluation to the broader Qwen3 family to conduct scaling analysis from 0.6B to 32B (see Appendix C.1). Evaluation Metrics. We evaluate models across two dimensions: capability and calibration. For capability, we report final-answer Accuracy. For calibration, we report Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Brier Score (BS) (Brier, 1950; Guo et al., 2017a; Desai & Durrett, 2020). To explicitly quantify the direction of miscalibration and the systematic optimism bias induced by post-training, we introduce the Overconfidence Gap (OCG), defined as the mean confidence minus the empirical accuracy. large positive OCG indicates severe overconfidence. Finally, to measure confidence discrimination, we introduce Strict Pairwise Ranking (SPR). SPR measures the probability that correct response receives strictly higher confidence score than an incorrect one (P(c+ > c)). Unlike AUROC, SPR assigns zero credit to ties, heavily penalizing the confidence saturation (e.g., uniformly predicting = 1.0) prevalent in OPD. More details are provided in Appendix C.2. Baselines. We evaluate CaOPD against two distinct categories of post-training methods. To illustrate the severe overconfidence degradation inherent to capability-focused post-training, we benchmark against state-of-the-art OPD paradigms (SDFT (Shenfeld et al., 2026), SDPO (Hübotter et al., 2026)) as well as standard RL baseline (GRPO (Shao et al., 2024)). To demonstrate CaOPDs superiority over specialized calibration techniques, we compare with calibration-aware RL methods, specifically RLCR (Damani et al., 2025) and CAR (Xuan et al., 2026), which explicitly penalize miscalibration via reward shaping. Detailed descriptions are provided in Appendix C.3. 6 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Model Domain Base (OCG) OPD CaOPD (Ours) SDFT (OCG) SDPO (OCG) SDFT (OCG) SDPO (OCG) Qwen3-8B Science Q&A 0.897 (+58.7%) 0.971 (+32.4%) Tool Use 0.972 (+48.1%) 0.996 (+32.0%) 0.935 (+12.9%) 0.973 (+31.1%) 0.382 (-11.8%) 0.699 (-0.7%) 0.770 (-3.3%) 0.652 (-5.7%) Olmo-3-7B-Instruct Science Q&A 0.831 (+49.3%) 0.911 (+36.7%) Tool Use 0.882 (+43.0%) 0.981 (+30.5%) 0.949 (+31.9%) 0.985 (+30.5%) 0.529 (+8.1%) 0.891 (+17.0%) 0.867 (+22.7%) 0.786 (+11.0%) Table 1: Mean confidence and OCG metrics across models, domains and methods. Accuracy is reported in percentages. Values in parentheses indicate the absolute change relative to the SDFT baseline. 5.2 Does On-Policy Distillation Inherently Exacerbate Overconfidence? OPD exacerbates baseline overconfidence. As shown in Table 1, base instruction-tuned models already exhibit severe optimism bias (e.g., massive +58.7% OCG for Qwen3-8B on Science Q&A). Strikingly, applying standard OPD methods (SDFT and SDPO) systematically worsens this pathology. Across almost all settings, OPD drives mean confidence toward extreme saturation (e.g., 0.996 on Tool Use), expanding the OCG (highlighted in red). This strongly validates our theoretical analysis: optimizing student behavior under privileged teacher context induces an optimism bias, forcing the model to blindly inherit absolute certainty without possessing the underlying evidence. CaOPD structurally realigns confidence. By decoupling capability imitation from confidence supervision, CaOPD successfully mitigates this dangerous drift. Replacing the teachers privileged confidence with the students empirical success drastically reduces mean confidence to realistic levels. Notably, for Qwen3-8B on Tool Use, CaOPD collapses the massive OCG from +32.0% (SDFT) down to an exceptionally aligned -0.7%. This demonstrates that CaOPD structurally corrects the confidence distribution, proving that true calibration can be explicitly learned during distillation. Figure 2: Optimization trajectories. CaOPD perfectly matches the accuracy improvements of standard SDPO (left) while simultaneously driving the calibration loss to convergence (middle), effectively bypassing the capability-calibration trade-off. The time per optimization step is nearly indistinguishable from SDPO (right), demonstrating that CaOPD introduces negligible latency during the gradient update phase. Values in parentheses indicate the absolute change relative to the SDPO baseline on the same backbone and domain. 5.3 Aligning Confidence Without Sacrificing Capability The next critical question: Can we explicitly calibrate the models confidence without damaging the reasoning capabilities acquired through privileged distillation? Overcoming OPD Saturation with Strong Discrimination. Table 2 and 3 compare CaOPD against SDFT, SDPO, GRPO and other baselines across different domains. CaOPD drastically reduces ECE and BS while maintaining or strictly improving task accuracy. Crucially, true calibration requires confidence to accurately rank correctness, which is measured by SPR. For instance, on Qwen3-8B Tool Use, standard SDFT yields highly low SPR of 0.085, indicating near-complete loss of discriminative power. CaOPD restores this metric to 0.555, proving it actively learns to assign strictly higher confidence to correct answers over mistakes. Avoiding the Capability Tax of RL-based Calibration. RL with explicit reward shaping is common alternative for taming overconfidence. However, as shown in Table 3, pushing calibration penalties into the RL reward fundamentally creates capability-calibration tradeoff, severely taxing the models task performance. On Qwen3-8B, Science Q&A, RLCR and CAR drop accuracy to 65.8% and 61.6%, respectively, lagging far behind GRPO (74.5%) and SDPO (80.6%). In contrast, CaOPD operates via direct target replacement rather than fighting the RL optimizer. It matches or exceeds the high capability of SDPO (e.g., boosting Tool Use accuracy from 66.2% to 70.9%) while achieving superior calibration metrics. Visualizing the Decoupling. Figure 2 explains why CaOPD bypasses this capability penalty. The left panel shows that CaOPDs accuracy trajectory perfectly shadows SDPO throughout training; the middle panel shows the calibration loss converging rapidly and stably. This confirms our core design principle: capability distillation and confidence calibration are algorithmically orthogonal. The model learns what to answer from the teachers context, while learning how certain it should be from the empirical target. Rollout Budget and Confidence Reshaping. We ablate the number of rollouts {1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32} (Appendix D.3, Figure 6). Task accuracy remains flat across all K, confirming that capability is fully decoupled from confidence sampling variance. For calibration, 8 emerges as compute-efficient sweet spot where the overconfidence gap collapses; smaller yields overly quantized targets that trap the model in overconfidence. Qualitatively, standard OPD collapses the confidence distribution into delta at 1.0; CaOPD restores dispersed distribution that the single-pass model internalizes, placing uncertain predictions into lower bins and restoring its capacity to differentiate successes from failures. 5.4 Generalization and Continual Learning common vulnerability of post-training calibration is overfitting to training-domain verbal patterns, causing failures on unseen tasks. To verify whether CaOPD learns robust 8 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Figure 3: Generalization of Calibrated Confidence. Evaluation on the Science Q&A (Chemistry)(Feng et al., 2024) test domain under (a) Out-of-Distribution shift (top row; Train: Tool Use Test: Chemistry) and (b) Continual Training (bottom row; Train: Tool Use Chemistry Test: Chemistry). Solid lighter bars represent In-Distribution (ID) baselines (trained on Chemistry), while hatched regions and black lines denote the absolute shift and final metric values after distribution changes. uncertainty representation, we evaluate it under Out-of-Distribution (OOD) shifts and Continual Training (CT). We employ the SDFT backbone, which was explicitly designed to enable continual learning (Shenfeld et al., 2026). Figure 3 illustrates the performance shifts on Qwen3-8B. Full results across all models are deferred to Appendix (Tables 7 and 8). OOD Generalization: Learning Meta-Skill. When transferring from Tool Use to Science Q&A(Feng et al., 2024) (Figure 3a), standard SDFT suffers complete calibration collapse. Despite maintaining reasonable OOD accuracy of 36.7%, its ECE spikes to massive 0.599, and its SPR plummets to 0.353. In contrast, CaOPD generalizes exceptionally well, achieving an OOD ECE of 0.358 (a 40% relative reduction) and maintaining healthy Overconfidence Gap. By grounding supervision in empirical rollouts, CaOPD avoids memorizing the confidence distribution of the source training domain, instead learning generalizable meta-skill: mapping its internal uncertainty to reliable verbalized confidence. Preventing "Calibration Forgetting" in CT. In the CT setting, models are first trained on Tool Use, then continually trained on Science Q&A (Figure 3(b)). Prior work showed SDFT successfully prevents the catastrophic forgetting of capabilities. However, we reveal critical new pathology: capability preservation does not guarantee calibration preservation (Figure 3b). SDFTs calibration is broken by sequential updates (ECE 0.513, SPR 0.230). CaOPD solves this "calibration forgetting." When trained continually, CaOPD matches SDFTs recovered accuracy but restores calibration, achieving CT ECE of 0.126 and an SPR of 0.662. 5.5 The Scaling Law of Miscalibration: Decoupling Capability from Calibration natural question is whether OPD-induced overconfidence is merely an artifact of smaller models. Does scaling up parameters automatically cure miscalibration? Scaling rigidly entrenches blind optimism. As demonstrated in our macro-level analysis of leading LLMs (Figure 1, left), we observe pervasive Scaling Law of Miscalibration: whether utilizing massive proprietary APIs or scaling up open-weight models, increasing capability (moving right along the X-axis) does not resolve the optimism bias. Models remain trapped in the overconfidence zone. To dissect this dynamically, Figure 4 tracks the Qwen3 family from 0.6B to 32B parameters. Under standard SDFT, mean confidence forms flat horizontal line saturated near 1.0. Whether the model has 0.6B or 32B parameters, OPD forces it into absolute certainty, rigidly cementing the information asymmetry. 9 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Figure 4: Decoupling Capability from Calibration across Model Scales. As Qwen3 scales from 0.6B to 32B (bubble size), standard SDFT (orange) fails to escape extreme overconfidence, remaining flat horizontal line near 1.0 (right). In contrast, CaOPD (green) dynamically aligns confidence with actual capability, completely dominating the Pareto frontier for Reliability (1-BS) (left) and Discrimination SPR (middle), proving successful decoupling. CaOPD achieves Pareto-optimal decoupling. CaOPD shatters this scaling curse. As models scale, CaOPD systematically pushes the Pareto frontier toward the top-right corner (Figure 4, left and middle), achieving strictly better Reliability and Discrimination (SPR). Crucially, CaOPDs mean confidence dynamically scales with its true accuracy, proving that capability distillation and calibration alignment can be safely decoupled. Ultimately, this decoupling enables compact 8B model to achieve calibration quality rivaling frontier LLMs (Figure 1).

sections.title: 6 Related Work
sections.content: On-Policy Distillation for Post-Training. One recent paradigm shift in the field of LLM post-training is trending toward "On-Policy Distillation" (OPD). (Agarwal et al., 2024; Lu & Lab, 2025; Ye et al., 2026) and self-distillation (Sang et al., 2026; Kim et al., 2026; Zhao et al., 2026; Askell et al., 2021; Snell et al., 2022; Choi et al., 2022). Methods such as Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO) (Hübotter et al., 2026), Self-Distillation Fine-Tuning (SDFT) (Shenfeld et al., 2026), and Self-Distilled Reasoner (On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD)) (Zhao et al., 2026) have demonstrated that models can bootstrap their own reasoning capabilities by acting as their own teachers. shared characteristic of these state-of-the-art frameworks is the use of privileged context such as environment verifier feedback, expert demonstrations, ground-truth solutions, or user interactions (Wang et al., 2026; Buening et al., 2026), to generate high-quality teacher trajectories. However, these works are overwhelmingly capability-centric. They focus on transferring the teachers reasoning accuracy while mathematically ignoring the information asymmetry between the privileged teacher and the deployed student. Our work is the first to formalize how this asymmetry inherently induces entropy collapse and systematic overconfidence, and we propose framework to fix this blind spot. Confidence Calibration and Reward Shaping. The epidemic of overconfidence in modern LLMs has been widely documented, prompting significant interest in calibration methods (Guo et al., 2017b; Kadavath et al., 2022a; Lin et al., 2022a; 2023; Tian et al., 2023; Groot & Valdenegro-Toro, 2024). The prevailing approach involves Reinforcement Learning (RL) augmented with explicit reward shaping. Works such as RLCR (Damani et al., 2025), Rewarding Doubt (Bani-Harouni et al., 2025), CAR (Xuan et al., 2026), and Taming Overconfidence (Leng et al., 2024) integrate proper scoring rules (e.g., Brier score penalties) directly into the PPO (Schulman et al., 2017) or RL objective to penalize overconfident mistakes. While effective at suppressing absolute confidence, these RL-based interventions often suffer from optimization instability and severe capability tax (Askell et al., 2021), the models become overly conservative, and their fundamental reasoning accuracy degrades to avoid penalties. In contrast, CaOPD avoids fighting the RL optimizer. By employing non-invasive target replacement strategy on the confidence token, CaOPD achieves Pareto-optimal alignment, decoupling capability distillation from confidence calibration without sacrificing capability. 10 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Test-Time Amortization and Hallucination. As recently highlighted by Kalai et al. (2025), modern language models are heavily incentivized to behave as aggressive "test-takers," opting to bluff or hallucinate rather than accurately signaling uncertainty (Kuhn et al., 2023a; Geng et al., 2024; Kirchhof et al., 2025). To detect and mitigate such hallucinations, substantial research has leveraged test-time multi-sampling. Frameworks like SelfCheckGPT (Manakul et al., 2023) and SAC3 (Zhang et al., 2023) estimate uncertainty by measuring the consistency across multiple independent generations. While highly reliable, these sampling-based approaches increase inference latency and compute costs by factor of O(K), making them prohibitive for real-time or agentic deployment. CaOPD acknowledges that multi-sampling yields the reliable uncertainty signal, but fundamentally shifts when this computation occurs. By calculating the empirical success rate during the data generation phase and distilling it into the model, CaOPD amortizes the expensive test-time search cost into single-pass inference model, resolving the hallucination-bluffing pathology with zero deployment (inference) overhead.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We identify pervasive pathology in modern LLM post-training: the Scaling Law of Miscalibration. We demonstrate, both theoretically and empirically, that standard OPD traps models in an illusion of certainty. To break this capability-calibration trade-off, we propose Calibration-Aware OPD (CaOPD), which decouples capability distillation from confidence supervision via simple target replacement mechanism. Extensive evaluations confirm that CaOPD collapses the Overconfidence Gap across various scales, domains, and distributional shifts, without incurring the capability tax typical of RL-based reward shaping. By enabling models to produce calibrated confidence in single forward pass, CaOPD provides practical foundation for deploying trustworthy, uncertainty-aware agents.

sections.title: 8 Limitations and Future Work
sections.content: Limitations. Like all on-policy distillation methods, CaOPD inherits the requirement that the base model possesses sufficient in-context learning capacity to benefit from the privileged teacher context z. Our scaling analysis (Section 5.5) shows that even at 0.6B parameters, CaOPD delivers meaningful calibration improvements; however, capability gains are naturally bounded by the base models reasoning capacity. Additionally, verbalized confidence requires the model to produce parsable confidence segment matching the specified format. While CaOPDs target replacement provides strong formatting signal during training (Appendix D.4), occasional formatting failures can occur at test time, limitation shared with all verbalized uncertainty methods and one we also observed in proprietary API evaluations. Finally, computing ˆµ(x) requires rollouts per prompt during training, increasing the training compute budget. We find = 8 sufficient in practice (Appendix D.3), and this cost is fully amortized at deployment where inference remains single O(1) forward pass. Future Work. Our work highlights several exciting directions for future research: Step-level calibration for long-horizon reasoning. CaOPD currently provides utterancelevel confidence. In agentic or multi-step CoT settings, extending the target replacement principle to dense, per-step uncertainty (e.g., integrating with Process Reward Models) could enable finer-grained error detection and early termination of flawed reasoning chains. Efficient rollout strategies. The current approach uses fixed rollout budget for all prompts. Adaptive allocation, e.g., early stopping when initial rollouts return unanimous verifier scores, could reduce training cost without degrading target quality. Extending CaOPD to preference optimization frameworks (DPO, RLHF) is also an open direction. Confidence-based cascade routing. As discussed in Appendix F, CaOPD-calibrated small models could serve as lightweight triage routers in cascade inference systems, deferring Preprint Salesforce AI Research uncertain queries to larger models. Empirically validating cost-accuracy tradeoffs under this architecture is natural next step.

This completes the proof. What this means for OPD: Proposition 1 formally explains the Information Gap introduced in Section 3.1. Because the teacher uses extra evidence (e.g., correct demonstration) to form its internal confidence, the student simply cannot "guess" this exact confidence value at test time. The best the student could theoretically do is predict its own marginal success rate µ(x). However, standard OPD minimizes the KL divergence point-wise on each trajectory, forcing the student to memorize the inaccessible µT(x, z) rather than learning the optimal projection µ(x). This structural mismatch guarantees that confidence distillation will fail to produce calibrated predictions. Takeaway 1: The Information Gap in Practice The mathematical impossibility of recovering the teachers exact confidence µT(X, Z) from alone means that standard OPD forces the student to chase "ghost" target. The proof demonstrates that the theoretically optimal strategy for the student is to predict its own marginal success rate µ(X). However, the standard OPD loss function prevents 18 Preprint Salesforce AI Research this, coercing the student to memorize inaccessible context cues. This structural mismatch mathematically guarantees that confidence distillation, if left coupled with capability cloning, will fail to produce calibrated predictions at deployment. In standard OPD, the model minimizes the per-token reverse KL divergence (Equation (2)). For given prefix y<t generated by the model in student mode, the per-token loss decomposes as: DKL πθ(v y<t, x) log (cid:0)πθ( y<t, x) (cid:13) (cid:13) πθ( y<t, x, z)(cid:1) = vV where is the token vocabulary. The reverse KL is mode-seeking: it heavily penalizes the model (in student mode) for placing probability mass on tokens where the privilegedconditioned distribution πθ( y<t, x, z) assigns low probability. Because the privileged distribution is sharply concentrated (as proven in Steps 13), the student-mode distribution πθ( y<t, x) is driven to collapse its mass onto the same narrow set of high-probability tokens. This forces the models internal logits to become artificially sharpened in order to match the teachers certainty, even though the model in student mode lacks access to the mutual information I(A; X) that justifies this concentration. This completes the proof. πθ(v y<t, x) πθ(v y<t, x, z) (17) , 19 Preprint Salesforce AI Research What this means for OPD: Proposition 2 formally explains the Entropy Collapse mechanism introduced in Section 3.1. Because the model under privileged conditioning possesses the cheat sheet (e.g., correct demonstrations or verifier feedback), its next-token distribution is highly concentrated (low entropy). The reverse KL objective forces the model in student mode to match this sharp distribution. Being mode-seeking, the reverse KL drives the model to collapse its probability mass onto the teachers high-confidence modes, drastically scaling up its internal logit magnitudes even though it lacks the evidence to justify such certainty. When the model is subsequently asked to verbalize its confidence, it translates this artificially low-entropy internal state into an unjustified, near-absolute confidence score (e.g., val(c) = 0.99). Takeaway 2: Artificial Sharpness and Logit Collapse This proof bridges information theory with model optimization dynamics. By demonstrating that H(A X, Z) < H(A X), we show that the model under privileged conditioning inherently operates with lower-entropy (sharper) distribution. Because the reverse KL is mode-seeking, it forces the model in student mode to concentrate its probability mass on the teachers high-confidence modes, artificially magnifying its internal logits during training. At test time, this manifests as extreme verbalized certainty. In essence, the model learns the magnitude of privileged-conditioned confidence without possessing the underlying mutual information I(A; X) that justifies it. What this means for OPD: Proposition 3 is the mathematical formalization of the Optimism Bias introduced in Section 3.1. Methods like SDFT and SDPO do not train on random garbage; they specifically construct to ensure that teacher has high-quality contexts. Consequently, the student is exclusively trained to imitate the highly certain, declarative linguistic style of successful derivation. The model perfectly learns the "voice of winner." At test time, when the student generates an incorrect answer due to its limited capability, it lacks the mechanism to express doubt. Instead, it blindly applies this inherited optimistic tone, resulting in the massive Overconfidence Gaps (OCG) we empirically observed in Table 1. Takeaway 3: The Origin of the Optimism Bias By evaluating the expectation over the successful subset of contexts (Dhelpful), the proof reveals systemic flaw in distilling only high-quality trajectories. Because the student is mathematically shielded from failure cases during supervision, it severely underestimates the complexity of the prompt distribution. Consequently, the student exclusively learns the declarative, confident tone of successful derivation. It blindly applies this inherited optimistic tone even on out-of-distribution or overly difficult test prompts, fully explaining the massive Overconfidence Gaps (OCG) we observe empirically.

sections.title: B Algorithm and Implementation Details
sections.content: B.1 The CaOPD Framework Pipeline To provide an intuitive understanding of the Calibration-Aware On-Policy Distillation (CaOPD) framework, Figure 5 illustrates the complete end-to-end pipeline used during the post-training data generation and optimization phases. The framework explicitly decouples capability learning from confidence alignment through five-step procedure: 1. Generate Verbalized Confidence: Given an input prompt x, the model (in student mode, i.e., conditioned only on x) generates complete trajectory = (a, c), where is the reasoning trajectory and is the confidence segment. In standard OPD, val(c) would typically be near 1.0, reflecting the teachers optimism inherited during training. 2. Approximate True Confidence: Instead of trusting the single-pass verbalized confidence, we estimate the students actual competence via empirical execution. We sample independent rollouts (ak, ck) πθ( x) and evaluate each with an objective task verifier to compute the empirical success rate ˆµ(x) = 1 k=1 R(x, ak). This serves as statistically rigorous, student-grounded approximation of the true deployment-time confidence. 3. Revise the Completion (Target Replacement): This is the core decoupling step of CaOPD. We take the students reasoning trajectory from Step 1, but explicitly discard its original confidence segment c. We construct the revised completion = (a, ˆµ(x)) by overwriting the confidence tokens with the string representation of the empirical success rate. This guarantees that the supervision target for confidence reflects the students actual competence rather than the teachers privileged certainty. 21 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Figure 5: Overview of the CaOPD Framework. The pipeline consists of five stages: (1) querying the student for base response with verbalized confidence, (2) approximating the true empirical confidence via student rollouts and an objective verifier, (3) revising the completion by replacing the original confidence tokens with the empirical success rate, (4) constructing the privileged teacher context with the same revised confidence, and (5) computing the per-token reverse KL between the student and teacher distributions on the revised completion. 4. Construct Revised Teacher Context: To ensure the model learns advanced reasoning capabilities, we construct privileged context by prepending the user question with environmental feedback, ground-truth demonstrations, or correct solutions, creating an open-book setting for the teacher. Crucially, the confidence score within originally near 1.0 is also overwritten with ˆµ(x), producing revised context z. This ensures that both the completion and the teachers conditioning reflect the same student-grounded confidence target. 5. Compute Reverse KL Loss: Both the student πθ( y<t, x) and the teacher πθ( y<t, x, z) perform forward pass on the revised completion y. The per-token reverse KL divergence (Equation (7)) is computed between these two distributions. As discussed in Section 4.2, this naturally decomposes into Capability Cloning component at reasoning positions and Confidence Calibration component at confidence positions. The detailed pseudocode for this entire process is formally summarized in Algorithm 1. Key Properties of CaOPD The CaOPD framework fundamentally restructures how posttrained LLMs learn to express uncertainty, exhibiting three desirable properties: True Decoupling of Behavior and Self-Assessment. Unlike RL methods (e.g., RLCR) that inject calibration penalties into global reward function often degrading task accuracy CaOPD operates via direct target replacement within the standard distillation pipeline. The loss (Equation (7)) preserves the teachers capability signal at reasoning positions while redirecting the confidence signal to the student-grounded target. Meta-Skill Generalization. Because CaOPD supervises confidence using the students actual execution outcomes rather than arbitrary heuristic penalties, the model learns generalizable meta-skill: mapping its internal uncertainty to an accurate verbalized scale. As demonstrated in Section 5.4, this allows calibrated confidence to generalize out-of-distribution and persist through continual training. Test-Time Compute Amortization. Obtaining reliable confidence via self-consistency or multi-sampling (i.e., computing ˆµ(x) online) is computationally prohibitive at deployment. CaOPD computes ˆµ(x) during the training phase via iterative on-policy rollouts. By distilling this signal into the models parametric weights, CaOPD amortizes the cost of test-time search into single-pass inference model. 22 Preprint Salesforce AI Research B.2 Verbalized Confidence Prompt Templates To elicit explicitly verbalized confidence scores from the language models, we adopt simple, plug-and-play prompting strategy. Rather than designing entirely new prompts, we seamlessly integrate standardized confidence instruction into the existing task-specific formatting templates. The core instruction appended to the formatting rules is: Confidence: your confidence level (0.0-1.0) regarding your answer, must be in numerical format, no other words or explanation. Below, we provide the full prompt templates and example model outputs for both the Science Q&A and Tool Use domains. B.2.1 Domain: Science Q&A (Chemistry) For the Science Q&A domain, the model is tasked with generating reasoning trajectory, selecting the correct multiple-choice option, and appending its confidence. 1 Given question and four options, please select the right answer. Respond in the following format: 2 <reasoning> 3 ... 4 </reasoning> 5 <answer> 6 ... 7 </answer> 8 Confidence: your confidence level (0.0-1.0) regarding your answer, must be in numerical format, no other words or explanation. 9 10 For the answer, only output the letter corresponding to the correct option (A, B, C, or D), and nothing else. Do not restate the answer text. For example, if the answer is "A", just output: 11 <reasoning> 12 ... 13 </reasoning> 14 <answer> 15 16 </answer> 17 Confidence: your confidence level (0.0-1.0) regarding your answer, must be in numerical format, no other words or explanation. 18 19 Begin! 20 Question: Which of the following molecules is the only correct reactant used in the synthesis of the product "Nc1ccn(Cc2ccc(Cl)cc2C(F)(F)F)" ? 21 A: O=C1c2ccccc2C(=O)N1c1ccn(Cc2ccc(Cl)cc2C(F)(F)F)n1 22 B: ... 23 C: ... 24 D: ... 25 Please reason step by step. To concretize this theoretical framework, we detail exactly how this privileged context is constructed in practice for both the SDFT and SDPO baselines, and crucially, where CaOPD intervenes to replace the confidence target. Target Replacement during CaOPD: By structuring the output with an explicit Confidence: [value] suffix, it becomes trivial to extract the scalar confidence score using rule-based parsing. During the CaOPD training phase (Algorithm 1), we perform two replacements: (1) in the students completion, we parse the confidence segment, drop the original numeric value, and overwrite it with the string representation of ˆµ(x); (2) in the teachers privileged context z, we likewise overwrite any confidence value (typically 1.0) with ˆµ(x), producing the revised context z. The per-token reverse KL loss is then computed on this revised completion under both the student conditioning (x) and the teacher conditioning (x, z). While the student is prompted strictly with the user query (Appendix B.2), the teacher receives heavily augmented prompt, the same query prepended with the privileged context z. We use colored annotations to highlight the exact token where CaOPD overwrites the default overconfidence with the students empirical target ˆµ(x). B.3.1 SDFT: Offline Demonstration Injection. B.4 Handling Target Non-Stationarity: Online Estimation of ˆµ(x) critical consideration in the CaOPD framework is the potential non-stationarity of the empirical confidence target. As the model πθ undergoes gradient updates and improves its reasoning capabilities, an empirical target ˆµ(x) computed from stale snapshot would rapidly become misaligned with the students true competence. To strictly prevent target staleness, our implementation of CaOPD does not rely on static, pre-computed offline dataset. Instead, ˆµ(x) is dynamically refreshed within an iterative, on-policy training loop. Specifically, the training dynamics proceed as follows: 1. EMA Teacher Tracking: In self-distillation, the teacher is the same model with different conditioning (Section 2.1). To stabilize the teachers distribution during training, we maintain an Exponential Moving Average (EMA) (Tarvainen & Valpola, 2017) copy of the model weights θema, updated at every gradient step: θema (1 α) θema + α θ. The teacher distribution is then πθema ( y<t, x, z), providing smoothed target for the reverse KL. 2. Dynamic Rollout Generation: Periodically during training, the latest model weights θ are synchronized to high-throughput inference engine (vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023)). The engine generates fresh batch of student rollouts (ak, ck) πθ( x) for each prompt on-the-fly. 3. Online Target Computation: These newly generated student rollouts are immediately evaluated by the objective task verifier R(x, ak). The empirical success rate ˆµ(x) = 1 k=1 R(x, ak) is computed strictly from this current batch of rollouts. 4. Target Replacement: The freshly computed ˆµ(x) is used to construct the revised completion and revised teacher context for the subsequent gradient optimization steps. This online, on-policy generation mechanism ensures that the supervision signal for confidence dynamically adapts to the models learning trajectory. As the model becomes more capable, the fresh rollouts yield higher success rate, and the target ˆµ(x) naturally scales upward. Consequently, CaOPD completely avoids the optimization instability associated with stale targets in standard off-policy algorithms. Note on Terminology: When we refer to the computational cost of rollouts being amortized offline in the main text, we specifically mean that this cost is absorbed prior to deployment (i.e., during the training phase), rather than requiring O(K) test-time compute from the end-user. B.5 Extended Discussion: Justification of the Empirical Target potential concern regarding the CaOPD framework is whether the sampling-based empirical frequency, ˆµ(x), serves as rigorous representation of the models true internal confidence, rather than merely heuristic proxy. We argue that ˆµ(x) is, in fact, the most statistically rigorous and unbiased target for confidence alignment, supported by both the mathematical definition of calibration and extensive literature on epistemic uncertainty. Preprint Salesforce AI Research Statistical Unbiasedness. By the strict definition of calibration, models predicted confidence should perfectly equal its expected accuracy on given prompt. For the deployed model πθ( x), its true expected success probability is exactly the marginal expectation over its own generation distribution: Eaπθ [R(x, a)]. Therefore, the Monte Carlo estimator ˆµ(x) = 1 k=1 R(x, ak) is statistically unbiased estimate of the models objective capability on query x. Training the model to produce val(c) = ˆµ(x) by replacing the confidence tokens in the distillation target (Section 4.2), ensures that the verbalized confidence honestly reflects the models own generation distribution. Connection to Behavioral Consistency and Hallucination Detection. Our formulation strongly aligns with established findings in the hallucination and uncertainty estimation literature. Extensive prior work has demonstrated that sampling-based behavioral consistency is the gold standard for measuring an LLMs epistemic uncertainty. For instance, Self-Consistency (Wang et al., 2022) leverages multiple rollouts to find the most reliable reasoning paths. More directly, hallucination detection frameworks such as SelfCheckGPT (Manakul et al., 2023) and SAC3 (Semantic-aware Cross-check) (Zhang et al., 2023) operate on the principle that when an LLM hallucinates (i.e., operates outside its knowledge boundary), its sampled responses exhibit high entropy and self-contradiction. Conversely, confident and factual knowledge yields highly consistent samples. CaOPD explicitly adopts this gold-standard consistency signal as the training target. However, while methods like SelfCheckGPT are applied at test time, incurring an expensive O(K) inference cost, CaOPD computes this signal during the training-phase data generation (Appendix B.4). By substituting it as the confidence target in the revised completion y, CaOPD amortizes the expensive test-time search cost into the models parametric weights, achieving self-consistency-level calibration in single O(1) forward pass. B.6 Extending CaOPD Without Objective Verifiers: Teacher-Anchored Self-Consistency potential critique of CaOPD is its reliance on an objective task verifier R(x, a) to compute the empirical target ˆµ(x). To address this, we first clarify the dependency structure of On-Policy Distillation (OPD), and then demonstrate how CaOPD can seamlessly operate in open-ended domains using Teacher-Anchored Self-Consistency. The Free Lunch of Verifier Dependency in OPD. It is crucial to recognize that the requirement for an objective verifier or ground-truth label is not uniquely introduced by CaOPD; it is fundamental prerequisite of the underlying OPD paradigms themselves. SDPO explicitly requires environment verifiers (e.g., compilers or reward models) to generate the privileged feedback z. SDFT requires ground-truth demonstrations to construct the privileged teacher context z. Because the base OPD algorithms already assume access to verification signals to construct the privileged context, CaOPD simply reuses this existing infrastructure to score the student rollouts, introducing zero additional assumptions or dependencies. Teacher-Anchored Self-Consistency. In scenarios where an objective verifier is strictly unavailable (e.g., open-ended alignment tasks), CaOPD can substitute R(x, a) with an unsupervised agreement metric. We propose Teacher-Anchored Self-Consistency. Instead of computing the majority vote among the students rollouts which is vulnerable to consistently wrong snowballing hallucinations we generate reference trajectory from the model under privileged conditioning, aref πθ( x, z), and compute the agreement rate between the student rollouts and this reference: ˆµSC(x) = (cid:16) 1 k=1 Equiv(ak, aref) (cid:17) (23) where Equiv(, ) is semantic equivalence function (e.g., LLM-as-a-Judge). This formulation solves the consensus hallucination problem: if the student consistently converges on false premise (e.g., 8 identical wrong answers), but the model under privileged conditioning provides the correct answer, the equivalence score remains exactly ˆµSC(x) = 0/8. The confidence target is properly set to 0.0, accurately reflecting the students inability to reach the correct state without privileged information. 29 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Method (Qwen3-8B) Science Q&A BS Acc. ECE Tool Use SPR Acc. ECE BS SPR SDFT (Baseline) CaOPD (Objective Verifier) CaOPD (TA-SC) 49.1 50.0 49.5 0.486 0.266 0.271 0.477 0.281 0. 0.387 0.599 0.577 67.6 70.6 68.3 0.321 0.228 0. 0.320 0.242 0.251 0.085 0.555 0.547 Table 4: Ablation study on the confidence supervision target using Qwen3-8B. TeacherAnchored Self-Consistency (TA-SC) serves as an unsupervised empirical target when an objective verifier is unavailable. While TA-SC experiences minor degradations due to teacher fallibility and spurious disagreements, it still drastically outperforms the standard SDFT baseline, effectively collapsing the overconfidence gap. Empirical Validation of TA-SC. To empirically validate the effectiveness of TeacherAnchored Self-Consistency (TA-SC), we conducted an ablation study using Qwen3-8B under the SDFT backbone. We replaced the exact objective verifier with the unsupervised TA-SC target. Specifically, we utilized rule-based exact-match for Science Q&A, and lightweight LLM judge to evaluate semantic equivalence for Tool Use. The results, summarized in Table 4, align with our theoretical expectations. While CaOPD with TA-SC exhibits slight degradation compared to the objective verifier (e.g., ECE increases marginally from 0.266 to 0.271 on Science Q&A), it substantially outperforms the standard SDFT baseline. The slight performance drop observed in the Tool Use domain (Accuracy decreases from 70.6% to 68.3%, SPR from 0.555 to 0.547) directly corroborates the Spurious Disagreements failure mode: open-ended JSON generations are inherently more susceptible to false-negative equivalence checks than rigid multiple-choice formats. Nevertheless, TA-SC drastically reduces the ECE (from 0.321 to 0.240) and restores discriminative ranking capability (SPR from 0.085 to 0.547) compared to standard distillation. This confirms that CaOPD is highly modular and maintains its decoupling even when relying on generative consensus in the absence of ground-truth verifiers. B.7 Logit-Based vs. Verbalized Calibration: The Decoupling of Internal Entropy fundamental question regarding the CaOPD framework is its choice of targeting explicitly verbalized confidence rather than internal token-level probability distributions (logits), and whether altering this verbalized output inadvertently corrupts the models internal reasoning confidence. Why Verbalized Confidence? As summarized in Table 5, estimating LLM uncertainty typically falls into three paradigms: logit-based (Kadavath et al., 2022b), sampling-based (Kuhn et al., 2023b; Wang et al., 2022), and verbalized confidence (Lin et al., 2022b). While logit-based methods (e.g., perplexity) are mathematically straightforward, they suffer from critical deployment limitations: they are entirely inaccessible for black-box proprietary APIs and require complex length-normalization for long-horizon reasoning trajectories. Sampling-based methods are robust but computationally prohibitive (O(K) latency) at test time. Verbalized confidence represents the ideal deployment paradigm it requires only single forward pass and operates perfectly via black-box API interactions. However, as documented in our work, its fatal flaw is that it is severely miscalibrated and overconfident by default after standard post-training. CaOPD specifically targets this paradigm because solving verbalized overconfidence unlocks the most scalable and universally applicable form of uncertainty signaling. Does Verbalized Humility Bleed into Internal Logit Entropy? natural question is whether training the model to output low verbalized confidence targets ( ˆµ(x) 0.0) inadvertently causes the internal token-level distributions at reasoning positions (t Ia) to 30 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Method Paradigm Mechanism Blackbox / API? Single Pass? Key Limitations Logit-based (e.g., Perplexity, Entropy) Leverages probability distribution over individual tokens Sampling-based (e.g., SAC3, Semantic Entropy) Examines semantic consistency across multiple generated samples Verbalized Confidence (e.g., P(True)) Asks the LLM itself to state its confidence inline via text Long responses require complex normalization; inaccessible via most commercial APIs. Computationally intensive at deployment; increases latency by factor of O(K). Overconfident by default under OPD; requires alignment to express honest doubt. Table 5: Comparison of Uncertainty Estimation Paradigms for LLMs. CaOPD targets Verbalized Confidence because it is the most deployment-friendly paradigm (API-compatible and single-pass), overcoming its inherent "overconfidence" limitation via rollout-grounded distillation. become flattened. In other words, does the induced humility at confidence positions destructively bleed into the models core reasoning capability? Our empirical training dynamics provide clear answer: No. The autoregressive structure of the generation = (a, ˆµ(x)) provides natural causal decoupling. The per-token reverse KL (Equation (7)) at reasoning positions Ia is computed on prefixes y<t that contain only reasoning tokens the confidence replacement has not yet appeared in the autoregressive context. Consequently, the teachers distribution at these positions remains anchored to the privileged reasoning signal from z, unaffected by the confidence replacement. The gradient adjustments pulling the confidence tokens toward ˆµ(x) are isolated to the final few sequence positions (t Ic). This decoupling is empirically confirmed by the left panel of Figure 2: CaOPDs accuracy trajectory perfectly shadows SDPO throughout training. If the low-confidence targets at Ic were corrupting the logit entropy at Ia, we would observe tangible capability degradation. Instead, the Transformer architecture successfully maintains sharp internal distributions for reasoning while selectively routing epistemic uncertainty into the allocated confidence tokens.

sections.title: C Additional Experimental Setup
sections.content: C.1 Datasets and Models To evaluate the confidence calibration of models post-trained via on-policy distillation (OPD), we conduct experiments across two distinct domains that require complex multistep reasoning and are highly susceptible to overconfidence: Science Question Answering (Science Q&A) and Tool Use. Science Q&A (Chemistry): We utilize the Chemistry L-3 subset from SciKnowEval (Feng et al., 2024) to test knowledge-intensive scientific reasoning. This dataset provides rigorous testbed for evaluating whether models confidence aligns with its factual accuracy in domain-specific contexts. Following standard practices, the data is partitioned into approximately 75% training, 5% validation, and 20% test splits. Tool Use: To assess calibration in long-horizon, sequential decision-making, we evaluate on the ToolAlpaca dataset (Tang et al., 2023). This domain requires the model to map user requests to the correct API specifications, testing its ability to remain calibrated across extended reasoning traces and execution steps. We follow the original train-test split provided by the authors. Following recent advancements in post-training and self-distillation, we select two representative open-weight language models as our primary testbeds: Qwen3-8B and Olmo3-7B-Instruct. This selection enables us to observe calibration behaviors across different model families and tuning paradigms under the same distillation pipeline. Furthermore, to systematically analyze the relationship between model scale, in-context learning (ICL) capability, and calibration effectiveness, we expand our evaluation to the broader Qwen3 Preprint Salesforce AI Research family. Specifically, we conduct comprehensive scaling analysis encompassing models at 0.6B, 1.7B, 4B, 8B, 14B, and 32B parameters. C.2 Extended Evaluation Metrics We introduce several metrics to comprehensively evaluate the capability and calibration of post-trained language models. Here, we provide their formal definitions and theoretical justifications. Overconfidence Gap (OCG). While standard proper scoring rules like Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Brier Score (BS) measure absolute conditional miscalibration, they obscure the direction of the error (i.e., whether the model is overconfident or underconfident). To explicitly quantify the systematic optimism bias induced by post-training, we track OCG, defined as the difference between the expected verbalized confidence and the empirical accuracy over the test set: OCG = E(cid:2)val(cθ(X))(cid:3) E[R] large positive OCG strictly indicates severe overconfidence, the primary pathology induced by standard OPD, whereas value near zero reflects well-calibrated marginal expectations. (24) Strict Pairwise Ranking (SPR) vs. AUROC. We introduce Strict Pairwise Ranking (SPR) to measure the discrimination ability of the models confidence. This metric is specifically designed to expose confidence saturation, severe pathology observed in on-policy distillation where models uniformly report maximum confidence (e.g., = 1.0) regardless of actual correctness. Let c+ denote the confidence score assigned to correct response, and denote the confidence score assigned to an incorrect response. The standard Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC) can be formulated probabilistically as: AUROC = P(c+ > c) + 0.5 P(c+ = c) (25) AUROC awards partial credit (0.5) when the confidence scores are tied (c+ = c). Consequently, completely saturated model that outputs = 1.0 for every single response will yield an AUROC of 0.5. While statistically representing "random guessing" baseline, this value often visually obscures the complete collapse of the models discriminative power. To strictly penalize this saturation, SPR removes the credit awarded for tied predictions: SPR = P(c+ > c) (26) Under SPR, tie yields 0 points. Therefore, if model uniformly predicts = 1.0, its SPR drops exactly to 0. This metric directly answers the most critical question for deployment: Does the model actually assign strictly higher confidence to its correct answers compared to its mistakes? By penalizing uninformative ties, SPR provides much more sensitive and realistic measure of confidence discrimination for post-trained language models. C.3 Detailed Baseline Descriptions To comprehensively evaluate our proposed CaOPD, we compare it against two distinct categories of post-training methods: capability-focused on-policy training and calibrationaware reinforcement learning (RL). 1. Capability-Focused Paradigms. These methods primarily optimize for task success (capability) and serve to empirically illustrate the severe overconfidence degradation typical in standard post-training pipelines. SDFT (Shenfeld et al., 2026) and SDPO (Hübotter et al., 2026): These represent the current state-of-the-art in on-policy self-distillation. The model generates its own trajectories and is trained via reverse KL against itself conditioned on privileged context (e.g., demonstrations or environment feedback). 32 Preprint Salesforce AI Research GRPO (Shao et al., 2024): GRPO serves as standard reinforcement learning baseline. It demonstrates the calibration effects of standard reward maximization without any specialized confidence penalties. 2. Calibration-Aware RL Methods. To demonstrate that CaOPD is superior not only to standard distillation but also to specialized calibration techniques, we benchmark against state-of-the-art methods that explicitly shape rewards to penalize miscalibration. RLCR (Damani et al., 2025): reinforcement learning approach that integrates proper scoring rules (such as the Brier score) directly into the PPO reward function to strictly penalize overconfident mistakes. CAR (Calibration-Aware Reward) (Xuan et al., 2026): Derived from the recent Confidence Dichotomy framework, CAR explicitly penalizes the dichotomy between high verbalized confidence and low accuracy. In our experiments, we adapted the RLCR training pipeline by incorporating CARs specialized calibration-penalized reward formulation. Comparing CaOPD against these RL-based interventions allows us to assess whether our student-grounded empirical target replacement provides fundamentally better capabilitycalibration trade-off than explicit, scalar reward shaping. C.4 Technical Setup and Hyperparameters Compute Infrastructure. All experiments were conducted on single compute node equipped with four NVIDIA H200 GPUs. Our software environment is built on top of the NVIDIA PyTorch container (nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:25.02-py3), utilizing CUDA 12.8 and PyTorch v2.7.0. To build CaOPD on SDFT, all experiments were conducted using the HuggingFace TRL (von Werra et al., 2020) library, while for developing on top of SDPO, the training pipeline is implemented based on the ver1 library (Sheng et al., 2025), and we employ PyTorch Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP2) for memory-efficient distributed training. For high-throughput rollout generation during the empirical target construction phase, we leverage vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023), which enables highly optimized batched inference on the multi-GPU node. Hyperparameters. The detailed hyperparameters used across our post-training runs for both Qwen3-8B and Olmo-3-7B-Instruct are summarized in Table 6.

sections.title: D Extended Results and Analysis
sections.content: D.1 Generalization and Continual Learning In Section 5.4, we presented the aggregated results demonstrating CaOPDs robustness under Out-of-Distribution (OOD) shifts and Continual Learning. Tables 7 and 8 provide the exhaustive metrics for these settings across the Qwen3-8B and Olmo-3-7B-Instruct backbones. Here, we provide the implementation details and expand on the underlying mechanisms driving these results. Implementation Details for Continual Training (CT). To rigorously evaluate the models in continual learning scenario, we adopt straightforward sequential fine-tuning approach without introducing domain-specific replay buffers or complex regularization terms. Specifically, we first train the base model on Domain (e.g., Tool Use) for the standard number of epochs to convergence. We then initialize the training for Domain (e.g., Science Q&A) using this exact checkpoint, and perform Continual Training (CT) on Domain for the exact same number of epochs and with identical hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, batch size) as the standalone ID training. This isolates the effect of the optimization objective (SDFT vs. CaOPD) on preserving or forgetting calibration. Why does standard OPD fail OOD? The massive calibration collapse observed in standard SDFT (e.g., OOD ECE soaring to 0.599 in Table 7) occurs because standard OPD forces the 33 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Parameter General & Data Base Models Thinking Mode Max prompt length Max response length Value Qwen3-8B, Olmo-3-7B-Instruct False 2048 8192 Batching & Rollout Question batch size Mini batch size Number of rollouts for empirical target (K) Inference engine Rollout Temperature Validation Number of rollouts Temperature Top-p 32 32 8 vLLM 1.0 {8,16} 0.6 0.95 Distillation Loss (SDPO / CaOPD) Top-K distillation Distillation divergence Teacher-EMA update rate Rollout importance sampling clip 100 Reverse-KL 0.05 2 Training & Optimization Optimizer Learning rate Warmup steps Weight decay Gradient Clip Norm AdamW {1 106, 1 105, 2 105 } 10 0.01 1. Table 6: Hyperparameters used for CaOPD. model to memorize the marginal confidence distribution of the source training domain. When shifted to an unseen OOD domain where the models intrinsic capability is strictly lower, the model blindly applies the highly confident linguistic tone (Optimism Bias) it memorized from the source domain. CaOPD prevents this by training the model to act as its own calibrator. Because the empirical target ˆµ(x) is dynamically generated based on the models actual competence on the current prompt, the model learns the functional mapping from its own hidden state entropy to confidence scalar, which robustly transfers across domains. The Mechanism of "Calibration Forgetting" in Continual Learning. In Continual Training, the models weights undergo sequential gradient updates to assimilate new knowledge. While self-distillation algorithms like SDFT are explicitly designed to prevent the catastrophic forgetting of reasoning capabilities (Accuracy), they completely fail to protect the calibration mapping. As shown in Table 8, SDFTs CT accuracy on Tool Use recovers perfectly to 0.647 (matching the ID baseline), but its SPR collapses completely to 0.000. This indicates complete loss of discriminative resolution for confidence. By continually enforcing the target replacement ( = (a, ˆµ(x)); Section 4.2) during sequential updates, CaOPD constantly re-anchors the confidence tokens to the models evolving capability frontier. This explicitly regularizes the confidence outputs against the models actual execution outcomes on the new domain, successfully immunizing the network against Calibration Forgetting. D.2 The True Cost of Calibration: Test-Time Compute vs. Amortization natural concern is that CaOPDs calibration gains come at the expense of additional compute. We clarify the cost structure at each stage. 34 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Method Setting Acc ECE Qwen3-8B BS SPR Mean Conf. Acc ECE BS SPR Mean Conf.
