For Accuracy and SPR, higher is better; for Brier Score (BS) and ECE, lower is better. Training-time overhead. CaOPDs gradient step is identical to standard SDPO or SDFT: the same token-level forward/backward pass on the revised completion under student and teacher conditioning. The only additional cost is computing ˆµ(x) from rollouts per prompt. Crucially, this cost depends on the OPD backbone: Under SDPO: SDPOs standard training loop already samples multiple rollouts per prompt to identify successful trajectories for constructing the privileged context z. CaOPD reuses these same rollouts to evaluate ˆµ(x), requiring only lightweight verifier pass on trajectories that have already been generated. The marginal sampling cost is therefore zero; the only addition is the verifier evaluation, which is negligible compared to generation. Under SDFT: The standard SDFT pipeline generates single student trajectory per prompt. CaOPD introduces additional rollouts for empirical confidence estimation. At = 8 (our default; see Appendix D.3 for the ablation), this increases rollout generation by constant factor. In practice, the overhead remains modest: as shown in Figure 2 (right), the wall-clock time per optimization step is nearly indistinguishable from the baseline, because rollout generation is parallelized via vLLM and does not bottleneck the training loop. Deployment-time savings. The key comparison is against test-time calibration methods such as Self-Consistency (Wang et al., 2022) or SelfCheckGPT (Manakul et al., 2023), which require generating responses online for every user query, incurring O(K) inference latency. CaOPD computes ˆµ(x) entirely during training and distills it into the models weights. At deployment, calibrated confidence is produced in single O(1) forward pass the same cost as an uncalibrated model. This makes CaOPD uniquely suited for latency-sensitive agentic deployment where per-query multi-sampling is prohibitive. Summary. Table 9 provides an overview of the cost structure. 35 Preprint Salesforce AI Research CaOPD + SDPO CaOPD + SDFT Extra rollout sampling Verifier evaluation Gradient step overhead Deployment cost None (reused) per prompt None O(1) per prompt per prompt None O(1) Table 9: Cost breakdown of CaOPD under different OPD backbones. Under SDPO, the rollouts required for ˆµ(x) are already generated by the base algorithm, so CaOPD adds only verifier evaluation pass. Under SDFT, additional rollouts are needed but the gradient step itself is unchanged. D.3 Detailed Analysis: Effect of Rollout Sample Size and Confidence Reshaping To provide deeper understanding of the compute-efficiency and distributional shifts induced by CaOPD, we expand upon the analysis presented in Section 5.3. Figure 6 presents both the ablation over the number of rollouts (K) and the resulting structural changes to the models confidence distribution. Effect of Rollout Sample Size (K). As shown in the left panel of Figure 6, we ablate {1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32}. Task accuracy (blue line) remains remarkably flat across all K, confirming that capability is completely decoupled from confidence sampling variance. Calibration exhibits phase transition: small (14) yields heavily quantized targets that trap the model in overconfidence, while = 8 emerges as compute-efficient sweet spot where mean confidence aligns with actual accuracy and discrimination (SPR) peaks. Beyond = 8, returns diminish rapidly. Why do small sample sizes (K 4) fail? The reason is target quantization. When = 1, the empirical target ˆµ(x) is strictly binary (0.0 or 1.0). This structurally prevents the model from learning intermediate confidence values, pushing it back toward the same saturation pathology as standard OPD. At = 8, the target space has 9 distinct levels (increments of 0.125), providing sufficient granularity to represent graded epistemic uncertainty. This also explains why CaOPDs Confidence Calibration term (Equation (7), Ic) requires non-degenerate target distribution: binary targets effectively reduce it to second copy of the saturation-inducing signal that CaOPD is designed to eliminate. Visualizing the Structural Reshaping of Confidence. The right panels of Figure 6 visualize the underlying confidence distributions. Standard OPD (SDFT eval) collapses the entire distribution into delta function at 1.0, destroying the models capacity for graded uncertainty. In contrast, the empirical targets used by CaOPD (K = 16, lower left) present dispersed distribution reflecting true execution variance across prompts. Crucially, the deployed single-pass CaOPD model (lower right) successfully internalizes this dispersion, actively placing uncertain predictions into lower confidence bins. This confirms that the target replacement does not merely shift the mean confidence downward but structurally restores the models ability to discriminate between successes and failures, consistent with the large SPR improvements reported in Tables 2 and 3. D.4 Training Dynamics and Format Adherence Across Model Scales To provide deeper understanding of how model capacity impacts the CaOPD optimization process, we track the training dynamics across the entire Qwen3 family (from 0.6B to 32B parameters). Figure 7 illustrates the training loss (left) and the confidence format adherence rate (right) over the course of training. Format adherence is defined as the fraction of rollouts that successfully generate parsable confidence segment matching the Confidence: [value] format specified in the prompt instructions (Appendix B.2). Optimization Efficiency Scales with Capacity. As shown in the left panel of Figure 7, convergence speed and terminal loss are strongly dictated by model size. Larger models (Qwen3-32B, 14B) converge rapidly (within 50 steps) to low loss, while smaller models (0.6B, 1.7B) exhibit higher variance and higher terminal loss. This confirms that internaliz36 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Figure 6: Effect of rollout sample size and confidence distributions. Figure 7: Training dynamics across model scales. Left: Larger models (14B, 32B) exhibit highly efficient optimization, rapidly converging to near-zero loss. Right: While small models (0.6B, 1.7B) initially struggle with the strict confidence formatting instructions, CaOPD acts as strong corrective alignment force, driving their format adherence to > 90% within the first 100 steps. ing the continuous empirical confidence target ˆµ(x), which takes values at granularities of 1/K (see Section D.3), requires sufficient representational capacity. Notably, despite these differences in convergence speed, even the 0.6B model achieves meaningful calibration improvements over its SDFT baseline (Figure 4), indicating that CaOPD provides value across the entire scale range. The Instruction-Following Bottleneck and CaOPDs Corrective Force. The right panel of Figure 7 provides empirical support for the constraints discussed in Appendix 8. Larger models inherently possess strong instruction-following capabilities, maintaining near 100% format adherence from step zero. Conversely, the 0.6B and 1.7B models initially struggle with the confidence formatting instruction, starting with adherence rates between 50% and 80%. However, CaOPD acts as corrective alignment mechanism: within the first 50 to 100 steps, the gradient updates drive even the 0.6B model to over 90% format adherence. This is direct consequence of the target replacement: since the revised completion always contains well-formatted confidence segment (the string representation of ˆµ(x)), the reverse KL loss at confidence positions (t Ic) provides consistent formatting signal at every training step. In contrast, standard OPD has no such structural anchor the confidence format is only implicitly encouraged by the prompt instruction, which small models may initially fail to follow. 37 Preprint Salesforce AI Research Model Type Accuracy ECE BS Mean Confidence GPT-5.2 GPT-5.4 Claude-Opus-4.6 Claude-Sonnet-4.5 Proprietary Proprietary Proprietary Proprietary Proprietary Gemini-2.5-Flash Gemini-2.5-Pro Proprietary Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite Proprietary Proprietary Gemini-3.1-Pro DeepSeek-V3.1 Kimi-K2.5 Qwen3.5-397B MiniMax-M2.5 Open-weight Open-weight Open-weight Open-weight Qwen3-8B (CaOPD) Ours 63.3% 72.9% 83.8% 72.4% 57.6% 61.9% 62.9% 71.9% 56.7% 60.0% 71.4% 47.6% 80.6% 0.106 0.080 0.027 0.155 0.424 0.365 0.360 0. 0.433 0.379 0.214 0.498 0.141 0.173 0.154 0.090 0.201 0.424 0.364 0.360 0.276 0.433 0.380 0.237 0.495 0. 0.608 0.802 0.838 0.874 1.000 0.984 0.989 0.997 1.000 0.979 0.929 0.974 0.770 Table 10: Detailed evaluation of leading proprietary and open-weight models on Science Q&A (Chemistry). Models are ordered by family. The massive gap between Accuracy and Mean Confidence in most models highlights the pervasive Optimism Bias induced by standard post-training. CaOPD enables compact 8B model to achieve calibration quality rivaling frontier APIs. D.5 Full API Evaluation Results and The Scaling Law of Miscalibration To substantiate the Scaling Law of Miscalibration introduced in Figure 1, we evaluate 12 leading LLMs spanning proprietary APIs (OpenAIs GPT-5.x series, Anthropics Claude 4.x series, Googles Gemini 3.1 series) and open-weight models (DeepSeek-V3.1, Qwen3.5-397B) on the Science Q&A (Chemistry) (Feng et al., 2024) domain. Table 10 presents the detailed capability and calibration metrics for all evaluated models. Pervasive Confidence Saturation. large number of models, particularly from the Gemini family (e.g., Gemini-3.1-Pro) and open-weight models (e.g., DeepSeek-V3.1), exhibit severe confidence saturation. Despite actual accuracies between 56% and 72%, their mean confidence scores are pegged at 0.991.00. This is consistent with our Entropy Collapse analysis (Proposition 2): on-policy distillation and RLHF force models to adopt the near-deterministic certainty of their privileged teacher distribution, leaving no capacity for expressing graded doubt. Calibration Requires Substantial Overhead in Current Pipelines. Among the proprietary APIs, only Claude-Opus-4.6 achieves near-perfect calibration (Accuracy: 83.8%, Mean Confidence: 0.838, ECE: 0.027). However, Opus-4.6 is massively scaled frontier model with undisclosed alignment pipelines. Standard-tier models from the same or other providers (e.g., Claude-Sonnet-4.5, Gemini-2.5-Pro) fail to replicate this calibration, suggesting that reliable uncertainty signaling currently demands prohibitive capability overhead or targeted calibration mechanism such as CaOPD. CaOPD Achieves Competitive Calibration at Small Scale. Our CaOPD-trained Qwen3-8B achieves 80.6% accuracy with well-aligned mean confidence of 0.770 (ECE: 0.141). Two caveats are important for fair interpretation. First, the high accuracy is primarily attributable to the SDPO backbone (which already achieves 80.6%; Table 3); CaOPDs contribution is the calibration improvement (ECE reduced from 0.157 to 0.141, SPR from 0.571 to 0.669) with no accuracy degradation. Second, the API models are evaluated zero-shot, whereas CaOPD is fine-tuned on the Chemistry domain, so the accuracy comparison is not strictly apples-to-apples. The meaningful takeaway is that CaOPD enables compact 8B model to achieve calibration quality (Reliability = 0.87; Figure 1, right) that rivals frontier APIs, demonstrating that explicit decoupling is far more efficient path to calibrated confidence than brute-force scaling. Preprint Salesforce AI Research Qualitative Case Studies: The Illusion of Certainty To concretely illustrate the severe overconfidence pathology of standard OPD and the corrective alignment of CaOPD, we present two qualitative examples from the ToolAlpaca (Tool Use) domain. These examples highlight the two core mechanisms of CaOPD: (1) preserving high confidence when the model is capable and correct, and (2) expressing honest doubt when the model operates outside its knowledge boundary. E.1 Case Study 1: Restoring Capability and Calibrated Confidence In this scenario, the model must select the correct API to find details about holiday. Standard SDFT falls into common agentic failure mode (over-planning/hallucinating search step) yet remains highly confident. CaOPD identifies the direct path and retains high, justified confidence. [Context] User Request: "I am curious about how the Chinese New Year is celebrated. Could you find the detailed information about the holiday, including its history, purpose, and traditions?" Available Tools: getHolidayDetails, searchHolidays, getHolidaysByCountry, etc. Ground Truth Action: getHolidayDetails with {"holidayId": "chinese-new-year"} [Standard SDFT] Incorrect & Overconfident Reasoning: "To find detailed information about the Chinese New Year, need to use the getHolidayDetails tool. However, first need to identify the unique identifier... will use the searchHolidays tool to find the relevant holiday ID." Predicted Action: searchHolidays (Incorrect tool selection) Verbalized Confidence: 0.95 [CaOPD (Ours)] Correct & Calibrated Reasoning: "To find detailed information about the Chinese New Year, need to use the getHolidayDetails tool... will assume they are interested in the general details of the holiday." Predicted Action: getHolidayDetails (Correct tool selection) Verbalized Confidence: 0.90 Takeaway: This example demonstrates that CaOPD does not merely apply naive, global downward penalty to the models confidence. When the CaOPD model successfully navigates the reasoning trajectory, it accurately assigns high confidence score (0.90). E.2 Case Study 2: Honest Failure and the "Illusion of Certainty" This example demonstrates "hard negative" where both models fail due to hallucinating optional API parameters. While SDFT exhibits blind optimism, CaOPD successfully signals its epistemic uncertainty. [Context] User Request: "Retrieve the text of Proverbs 18:24 from the Bible Digital API." In contrast, CaOPDs confidence score of 0.10 accurately flags the models uncertainty about the optional parameters. In production environment, this low signal would safely trigger human-in-the-loop fallback or an API schema reevaluation. This behavior is direct consequence of the target replacement mechanism (Section 4.2): during training, the models rollouts on similar prompts would have yielded low empirical success rate ˆµ(x), which was distilled as the confidence target. Broader Impact: Calibrated Small Models as Cascade Routers In Section 5.5 (Figure 4), we demonstrated that CaOPD decouples capability from calibration across model scales from 0.6B to 32B. An interesting consequence emerges at the lower end of the scaling curve: small models trained with CaOPD can accurately signal their own limitations. As shown in the rightmost panel of Figure 4, the CaOPD-trained 0.6B model achieves approximately 58% accuracy, and its mean confidence drops to roughly 0.3, faithfully reflecting this limitation. In contrast, the SDFT-trained 0.6B model outputs confidence of 1.0 despite failing nearly half the time. This distinction is not merely cosmetic: confidence score of 1.0 on 58%-accurate model provides zero signal for downstream decision-making, while score of 0.3 is actionable. Application: Confidence-Based Cascade Routing. This property enables practical deployment pattern. In cascade inference system, CaOPD-trained small model is deployed at the frontend. For queries where the models confidence exceeds threshold, it serves the answer at low cost and latency. For queries where confidence is low, the system defers to larger, more expensive model. The key requirement for such system is that the routers confidence must be calibrated, otherwise the threshold is meaningless. Standard OPD models, which saturate at = 1.0, cannot serve as routers regardless of their accuracy. CaOPD structurally resolves this by ensuring that confidence scales with actual capability (Figure 4, right panel). We note that this discussion identifies promising application direction; empirically validating cascade routing performance (e.g., cost-accuracy tradeoffs under different thresholds) is left to future work.
